methodology_sections: 1. Lifecycle Integrity - State transitions, illegal states, predecessors
2. Monotonicity & Irreversibility - Increasing values, one-way transitions
3. Access & Permission Logic - Conditional access, role requirements
4. Time & Resource Integrity - Time-locks, resource bounds, expiry

invariants.description: The execute() function allows execution of proposals in Defeated and Expired states, not just Succeeded. Any proposal that completes voting—regardless of outcome—can be executed, completely undermining governance.
invariants.attack_scenario: Attacker creates a malicious governance proposal. The community votes it down (Defeated) or it fails quorum (Expired). Attacker calls execute() anyway and the malicious actions are performed, since the require allows Defeated and Expired states.
invariants.invariant_logic: execute(proposalId) REQUIRES state(proposalId) == Succeeded; Defeated and Expired proposals MUST NOT be executable
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_onlySucceededExecutable(uint256 proposalId) public view { if (_proposals[proposalId].executed) { /* at the moment executed was set, state should have been Succeeded */ assert(false); /* cannot check retroactively, but execute should require state == Succeeded only */ } }

invariants.description: setRouter() has an inverted zero-address check: require(_router == address(0)) instead of require(_router != address(0)). This means the only value that can be set is address(0), permanently bricking the ability to update the router and potentially bricking all genesis pool launches if the router is set to zero.
invariants.attack_scenario: Owner calls setRouter(validRouterAddress) which reverts. Owner calls setRouter(address(0)) which succeeds, setting router to zero. All subsequent launch() calls use address(0) as router, reverting and permanently preventing any genesis pool from launching.
invariants.invariant_logic: router != address(0) after any successful call to setRouter; setRouter SHOULD reject address(0) not accept only address(0)
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_routerNotZero() public view { assert(router != address(0)); }

invariants.description: In the Governor.sol (smNFT-based) L2GovernorVotesQuorumFraction, the quorum() function ignores the blockTimestamp parameter and uses getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without a timestamp argument, meaning quorum is always calculated against current total supply rather than supply at proposal snapshot time. This allows quorum manipulation after proposal creation.
invariants.attack_scenario: Attacker creates a proposal when smNFT total supply is high (quorum is high). Community votes but barely meets quorum. Attacker then causes supply to increase (e.g., more smNFTs minted), retroactively raising the quorum threshold. The proposal that met quorum at vote time now fails quorum check, preventing execution of legitimate passed proposals. Alternatively, supply decrease after creation lowers quorum, allowing weak proposals to pass.
invariants.invariant_logic: quorum(blockTimestamp) MUST use supply at blockTimestamp, not current supply; quorum for a given proposalId should be fixed at proposal creation snapshot
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_quorumUsesSnapshot(uint256 proposalId) public view { /* quorum(proposalSnapshot(proposalId)) should return consistent value based on historical supply, not current */ }

invariants.description: The depositToken() function in GenesisPoolManager does not validate that the genesisPool address was actually created by the GenesisPoolFactory. An attacker can deploy a malicious contract implementing IGenesisPool that returns controlled values, triggering _preLaunchPool() which creates a gauge for an arbitrary pair address.
invariants.attack_scenario: Attacker deploys a FakeGenesisPool contract that: (1) returns true from depositToken (indicating preLaunch eligibility) without transferring any tokens, (2) returns an attacker-controlled pair address from getLiquidityPoolInfo(). Attacker calls GenesisPoolManager.depositToken(fakeGenesisPool, 1). The manager creates a gauge via gaugeManager.createGauge() for the attacker's pair, potentially redirecting emissions to an unauthorized pool.
invariants.invariant_logic: For all calls depositToken(genesisPool, amount): genesisPoolFactory.getGenesisPool(nativeToken) == genesisPool for some valid nativeToken
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_genesisPoolIsLegitimate(address genesisPool) public view { /* There must exist a nativeToken such that genesisFactory.getGenesisPool(nativeToken) == genesisPool */ }

invariants.description: The setPoolStatus() function (callable by manager) performs no validation of the state transition. Any status can be set from any current status, violating the intended lifecycle: DEFAULT→NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED→PRE_LISTING→PRE_LAUNCH→PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED→LAUNCH/PARTIALLY_LAUNCHED. A compromised or buggy manager could set LAUNCH without actually launching, trapping user deposits permanently.
invariants.invariant_logic: setPoolStatus(newStatus) MUST validate: (PARTIALLY_LAUNCHED requires current == PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED), (LAUNCH requires current == PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED), (NOT_QUALIFIED requires current in {NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED, PRE_LISTING}); No backward transitions except to NOT_QUALIFIED
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_validTransition(PoolStatus oldStatus, PoolStatus newStatus) public pure { if (newStatus == PoolStatus.LAUNCH) assert(oldStatus == PoolStatus.PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED); if (newStatus == PoolStatus.PARTIALLY_LAUNCHED) assert(oldStatus == PoolStatus.PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED); }

invariants.description: The _addLiquidityAndDistribute function passes 0 for both amountAMin and amountBMin when calling addLiquidity. Since genesis pool launches are predictable (triggered at epoch flip by epochController), an MEV attacker can front-run the launch transaction to manipulate the pool reserves, extracting value from the liquidity provision.
invariants.attack_scenario: Attacker monitors for the epoch flip transaction that triggers _launchPool. Before it executes, the attacker front-runs by depositing into the pair to skew the ratio. The addLiquidity call with (0,0) min amounts accepts the skewed ratio, giving fewer LP tokens than expected. The attacker back-runs to extract the profit. Genesis pool depositors receive less liquidity value than their deposits warrant.
invariants.invariant_logic: addLiquidity(amountAMin, amountBMin) MUST have amountAMin > 0 AND amountBMin > 0, computed as a function of allocatedNativeAmount and allocatedFundingAmount with acceptable slippage
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_nonZeroSlippage() public pure { /* amountAMin and amountBMin in addLiquidity call must be > 0 */ assert(false); }

invariants.description: In Governor.sol's L2GovernorCountingSimple, _voteSucceeded requires forVotes > againstVotes AND forVotes > abstainVotes. This means that even if a majority votes For (over Against), a large number of Abstain votes can cause the proposal to neither Succeed nor be Defeated, landing in Expired state. Combined with INV-STATE-001, Expired proposals can still be executed.
invariants.attack_scenario: A contentious proposal has 100 forVotes and 50 againstVotes and 150 abstainVotes, with quorum reached. forVotes > againstVotes (would normally succeed) but forVotes < abstainVotes, so _voteSucceeded returns false. _voteDefeated also returns false (againstVotes < forVotes). State becomes Expired. Yet execute() allows executing Expired proposals, so the proposal executes despite unclear mandate.
invariants.invariant_logic: _voteSucceeded(proposalId) SHOULD only require forVotes > againstVotes (abstain should not count against success); OR execute() must REJECT Expired proposals
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_voteSucceededConsistency(uint256 proposalId) public view { (uint256 against, uint256 _for, uint256 abstain) = proposalVotes(proposalId); if (_for > against) { assert(_voteSucceeded(proposalId) == true); } }

invariants.description: The depositToken function allows deposits in both PRE_LISTING and PRE_LAUNCH states, checking only that block.timestamp >= startTime. It does not enforce the deposit window end time (startTime + duration - NO_GENESIS_DEPOSIT_WINDOW). The end-of-window enforcement relies entirely on the manager transitioning to PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED during checkBeforeEpochFlip. If checkBeforeEpochFlip is not called in time, deposits continue past the intended window.
invariants.invariant_logic: depositToken MUST revert if block.timestamp >= genesisInfo.startTime + genesisInfo.duration - NO_GENESIS_DEPOSIT_WINDOW
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_depositWindowEnforced() public view { if (poolStatus == PoolStatus.PRE_LAUNCH || poolStatus == PoolStatus.PRE_LISTING) { assert(block.timestamp < genesisInfo.startTime + genesisInfo.duration - BlackTimeLibrary.NO_GENESIS_DEPOSIT_WINDOW); } }

invariants.description: After transferIncentives sends incentive tokens to the external bribe via notifyRewardAmount, the incentives mapping still holds non-zero values. If the pool later transitions to NOT_QUALIFIED (e.g., via manager's setPoolStatus), claimableIncentives returns non-zero amounts, and claimIncentives would attempt to transfer tokens the pool no longer holds. While SafeERC20 would revert, it creates a permanently stuck state where tokenOwner sees claimable amounts but can never claim.
invariants.attack_scenario: Genesis pool goes through: NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED→PRE_LISTING (incentives added)→PRE_LAUNCH (incentives transferred to bribe via transferIncentives). If manager then calls setPoolStatus(NOT_QUALIFIED), tokenOwner sees claimableIncentives returning non-zero amounts but every claimIncentives call reverts because pool has zero balance of those tokens. Incentive tokens are permanently unrecoverable by the tokenOwner.
invariants.invariant_logic: After transferIncentives completes: for all tokens t in incentiveTokens, incentives[t] == 0
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_incentivesZeroAfterTransfer() public view { if (poolStatus >= PoolStatus.PRE_LAUNCH) { for (uint i = 0; i < incentiveTokens.length; i++) { assert(incentives[incentiveTokens[i]] == 0); } } }

invariants.description: The deductAmount function for tokenOwner has an arithmetic edge case: userAmount is computed as (totalDeposits * gaugeTokenAmount) / _depositerLiquidity even when the tokenOwner portion should not deduct from userDeposits. In the else branch, the subtraction userAmount -= (totalDeposits * pendingOwnerStaked) / _depositerLiquidity could result in a value that exceeds userDeposits[account] due to rounding, causing underflow revert and permanently preventing the tokenOwner from unstaking.
invariants.attack_scenario: Token owner who is also a depositor tries to unstake a specific amount. Due to integer division rounding, userAmount after the subtraction becomes slightly larger than userDeposits[account], causing the final userDeposits[account] -= userAmount to underflow and revert. The owner's LP tokens become permanently locked in the gauge.
invariants.invariant_logic: deductAmount(account, gaugeTokenAmount): the computed userAmount after adjustments MUST be <= userDeposits[account]
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_deductNeverUnderflows(address account, uint256 gaugeTokenAmount) public view { uint256 _depositerLiquidity = liquidity / 2; uint256 userAmount = (totalDeposits * gaugeTokenAmount) / _depositerLiquidity; /* after adjustments */ assert(userAmount <= userDeposits[account]); }

invariants.description: The depositNativeToken function allows setting an auction that could be address(0) if auctionFactory.auctions(auctionIndex) returns address(0) and auctionFactory.auctions(0) also returns address(0) (both were removed). This null auction address is passed to setGenesisPoolInfo without validation, causing all subsequent depositToken calls to revert when calling auction.getNativeTokenAmount.
invariants.attack_scenario: Admin removes auction at index 0 via auctionFactory.removeAuction(0). User calls depositNativeToken with auctionIndex=0. auctions(0) returns address(0), fallback also returns address(0). setGenesisPoolInfo stores address(0) as auction. Any user calling depositToken will revert because _getNativeTokenAmount calls IAuction(address(0)).getNativeTokenAmount which reverts. Genesis pool is bricked.
invariants.invariant_logic: auction address stored in GenesisPool MUST NOT be address(0); setGenesisPoolInfo SHOULD validate auction != address(0)
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_auctionNotZero() public view { assert(address(auction) != address(0)); }

invariants.description: The balanceOf function divides liquidity by 2 to split between depositors and tokenOwner. When liquidity is odd, 1 wei of LP token is unaccounted for (lost to rounding). More critically, if totalDeposits is 0 (no depositors), the division by totalDeposits in balanceOf will revert with division by zero for any non-owner account query after launch.
invariants.attack_scenario: A genesis pool launches with allocatedNativeAmount == proposedNativeAmount (complete launch) but due to the auction math, it's possible the funding was provided by very few depositors. If totalDeposits somehow becomes 0 after deductAllAmount is called for the last depositer, any subsequent balanceOf query for a new address divides by zero and reverts, breaking integrations that query balances.
invariants.invariant_logic: balanceOf MUST NOT divide by zero; if totalDeposits == 0, depositor share must return 0 without reverting
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_balanceOfNoRevert(address account) public view { if (liquidity > 0 && totalDeposits == 0 && account != genesisInfo.tokenOwner) { /* balanceOf would revert with div by 0 */ assert(false); } }

invariants.description: The checkBeforeEpochFlip function updates pre_epoch_period only after processing all live tokens. If the loop reverts for any token (e.g., due to a malicious or broken genesis pool), pre_epoch_period is never updated. On the next call, the same tokens are processed again. A single broken genesis pool can block the entire epoch flip mechanism for all genesis pools.
invariants.attack_scenario: A genesis pool's external contract call (e.g., pairFactory.setGenesisStatus) reverts due to an edge case. checkBeforeEpochFlip reverts before updating pre_epoch_period. All subsequent checkBeforeEpochFlip calls re-enter the same failing loop. No genesis pools can transition to PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED or be disqualified, blocking the entire genesis pool lifecycle.
invariants.invariant_logic: A single genesis pool failure in checkBeforeEpochFlip MUST NOT block processing of other genesis pools
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_epochFlipProgress() public view { /* pre_epoch_period should advance every WEEK regardless of individual pool failures */ assert(block.timestamp < pre_epoch_period + 2 * WEEK); }

invariants.description: The claimNative function allows claiming refundable native tokens in NOT_QUALIFIED state, but the incentives that were already transferred to the external bribe (via transferIncentives) cannot be recovered. The state machine allows a pool to reach PRE_LAUNCH (incentives transferred) and then be set to NOT_QUALIFIED via setPoolStatus, leaving incentives irrecoverable by the token owner.
invariants.invariant_logic: If poolStatus transitions from a post-incentive-transfer state to NOT_QUALIFIED, incentive tokens already sent to external bribe MUST be recoverable by tokenOwner
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_incentivesRecoverable() public view { if (poolStatus == PoolStatus.NOT_QUALIFIED) { for (uint i = 0; i < incentiveTokens.length; i++) { if (incentives[incentiveTokens[i]] > 0) { assert(IERC20(incentiveTokens[i]).balanceOf(address(this)) >= incentives[incentiveTokens[i]]); } } } }
